Patch Set 4:

> If possible, I'd like to put this patch on hold because I really don't want to have a `short-circuit' IO path in CLIO.

Jinshan, could you please elaborate? Do you think that pages used by lockless/direct I/O should still be submitted through osc_oap_to_pending/osc_send_oap_rpc? If so, this really changes the design of the new RPC engine i'm working on in ORI-255.

Patch Set 5:

Niu, Jinshan, have we finally come to an agreement on this patch? Do we want to go ahead and land it or do we want to keep the direct i/o path common with buffered i/o as suggested by Jinshan in a previous comment?

Patch Set 5:

Jingshan is working on the review. 

From my point of view, two problems are hard to be resolved if we make dio & buffered io using same io path:
- Unaligned dio. (current dio must be page size aligned, which looks too restricted for the applications.)
- Overhead of page copy in lockless io.

Patch Set 5: I would prefer that you didn't submit this

(10 inline comments)

This patch is generally good. You still need to work on this:
1. We don't need CPT_TRANSIENT any more;
2. osc_lock_lockless_cancel() should be changed to make sure that it doesn't cover any pages;
3. please remove oi_lockless checking in osc_io_{prepare|commit}_write() because it's impossible for lockless IO to get there.

Thanks.

Patch Set 5: (10 inline comments)

{quote}
This patch is generally good. You still need to work on this: 1. We don't need CPT_TRANSIENT any more; 2. osc_lock_lockless_cancel() should be changed to make sure that it doesn't cover any pages; 3. please remove oi_lockless checking in osc_io_{prepare|commit}_write() because it's impossible for lockless IO to get there.
{quote}
Thanks for your review, Xiong.

1. CPT_TRANSIENT page is still used for liblustre & echo client.
2. I'm not sure if I follow you on this.
3. Sure, thanks.

Patch Set 5: (6 inline comments)

I don't know if we should leave CPT_TRANSIENT in liblustre and echo_client, or change them to use new DIO interfaces.

For osc_lock_lockless_cancel() stuff, now that we don't cache any page for lockless IO, it can be an empty function(I prefer to verify that no pages is being covered).

Patch Set 5:

Hi, Andreas

We are going to rewrite the dio code path, however, the old direct io functions is still used by lloop, I remembered that you mentioned that lloop isn't maintained anymore, so what should we deal with the old functions used in lloop? Replace them with new functions or just remove the whole lloop? Any suggestions? Thank you.

Patch Set 18: Looks good to me, but someone else must approve
Patch Set 18: Looks good to me, but someone else must approve

Patch Set 18:

I may think of the following performance issues with this patch:

1. DIO will not gather cached BIO pages to its RPCs, so to send all such pages, new RPC will be needed. it may slightly decrease performance, although not critically.
2. DIO will not find cached pages on the client side anymore, and will re-read them from OST again - such case will be slower.
3. mixed IO happened on the same region, could minimize the fragmentation in this IO and therefore the amount seeks on OST, if sent together. DIO sent separately, will not care about it, so the IO performance on OST will be slower due to amount of seeks. however, this is a very very rare case, so I would not pay much attention to it.
4. DIO and BIO sent through separate engines may lead to well known 1.8 problem, with DIO generates a lot of RPCs, but does not pay attention on max dirty pages and grants; when max dirty pages is reached, BIO starts generating RPC, but its slow due to large amount of DIO RPCs, and new BIO is just blocked, whereas DIO IO is very active - the patch will add this misbalance back again. 

therefore, I think (2) and (4) should be addressed before landing this patch.

Patch Set 18:

Hi, Vitaly

for 2: I don't think direct read should check page cache, DIO means it should bypass page cache, and this patch is doing the right thing. If DIO read uses cached page, what's the difference of direct read with buffered read? In most case, I think DIO user manages cache in userspace by itself, lookup page cache just added unnecessary overhead.

for 4: I don't think there will be heavily mixed DIO and buffered IO in the real world (Did any 1.8 user report such problem?). And actually, if we can tolerate DIO fails for ENOSPC when there is still available grant on the client, the grant consume code for DIO can be totally removed.

Patch Set 18:

Indeed, like we discussed with Vitaly already, we should not be looking for cahced pages for direct IO at all.
In fact kernel goes to great pains to flush all such cached pages before starting direct IO, so there should not be any pages like that anyway (unless there's racing IO).

Mixing Direct io and non-direct IO in the same RPC is also somewhat bad idea because generally we want to commit direct io right away on the server, but non-direct io could wait. (and besides, this is a pretty stupid usecase that I don't think needs any optimization).

Patch Set 18:

"Mixing Direct io and non-direct IO in the same RPC is also somewhat bad idea because generally we want to commit direct io right away on the server, but non-direct io could wait. (and besides, this is a pretty stupid usecase that I don't think needs any optimization)."

IO engine doesn't allow to mix DIO and cache IO pages in the same RPC.

It's probably not necessary to use grant for DIO because grant is used to solve ENOSPC problem but DIO won't have this problem at all. This is actually an endless discussion. I've involved in this kind discussion several times ;-).

The reason I agree with this patch is that it can simplify Client IO a little bit because it can get rid of TRANSIENT pages.

Patch Set 18: I would prefer that you didn't submit this

parents.subject: LU-3432 llite: Access to released file trigs a restore

commit.subject: LU-247 clio: Unaligned dio support
commit.message:
LU-247 clio: Unaligned dio support

- Port unaligned dio support (b18801);

- Changed the buffered lockless io into direct lockless io, since
  it doesn't make sense to copy the data between page cache and user
  buffer for lockless io, and the overhead of such copy sometimes
  could be heavy (as shown in b18801). Furthermore, dirty page cache
  without holding dlm lock could result in cache inconsistence.

- Fix the problems caused by concurrent dio read & buffered read,
  like LU-3192.

Signed-off-by: Niu Yawei <niu@whamcloud.com>
Change-Id: Ic9aebbd5fcafddfd9ce5c43c980ec79c0b54bbf9
